key_insight: A fully differentiable, JAX-based finite element framework can run finite-strain anisotropic plasticity simulations on GPUs and use automatic differentiation to efficiently identify material parameters from full-field data.
engineering_relevance: This enables faster forward analysis and practical inverse characterization of complex constitutive models for advanced manufacturing, reducing reliance on manual derivations and costly finite-difference sensitivity calculations.
method: Automatic differentiation through constitutive update and solver
Inverse optimization using heterogeneous, topology-optimized specimens with displacement fields
evidence_signal: On a 3-million-DOF forward problem, a single NVIDIA H100 achieved up to 9.4× speed-up over a 24-core CPU Abaqus baseline, and the framework recovered anisotropic yield and hardening parameters in multiple inverse studies.
watch_item: Performance and scalability depend on GPU memory, sparse solver efficiency, and the fidelity of the constitutive model and experimental full-field data.
paper_title: A Differentiable GPU-Accelerated Finite Element Framework for Inverse Characterization of Finite-Strain Anisotropic Plasticity
